assumption_register.assumption: modernc.org/sqlite is suitable for the full workload (concurrent reads, WAL mode, FTS5, recursive CTEs, STRICT tables)
assumption_register.mitigation: modernc.org/sqlite supports all listed features. Sharp edges document known quirks (MaxOpenConns, deferred TX). Well-verified.
assumption_register.risk_if_false: Would need to switch to mattn/go-sqlite3 (CGO) or ncruces/go-sqlite3 (WASM), breaking no-CGO constraint
assumption_register.verification_method: Unit tests with in-memory SQLite + benchmarks at 10K node scale

assumption_register.assumption: Sub-100ms retrieval is achievable on 10K-node graphs with recursive CTEs limited to depth 4
assumption_register.mitigation: Agent already documents gonum fallback for >10K edges. Benchmark tests prescribed.
assumption_register.risk_if_false: Graph traversal becomes the bottleneck; would need to pre-compute neighborhoods or use in-memory gonum graphs
assumption_register.source: System Constraints table, line 98; Focus Area 3 rules, line 311
assumption_register.verification_method: BenchmarkBFSTraversal with 10K nodes, varying edge density (sparse/dense/power-law)

assumption_register.assumption: chromem-go is suitable for <100K vectors with sub-100ms search
assumption_register.mitigation: chromem-go README claims '100K in ~40ms'. Needs independent benchmark verification. Fallback documented.
assumption_register.risk_if_false: Would need coder/hnsw (AGPL-3 issue) or custom implementation
assumption_register.source: Approved Dependencies table, line 109; sharp edge sqlite-vec-modernc-incompatible
assumption_register.verification_method: Benchmark with 100K 768-dimensional vectors, measure P99 search latency

assumption_register.assumption: Obsidian vault format (wikilinks, frontmatter, markdown) is stable and well-documented
assumption_register.mitigation: Obsidian format has been stable for 4+ years. goldmark parser libraries are mature.
assumption_register.risk_if_false: Vault output becomes incompatible with Obsidian; user-facing breakage
assumption_register.source: System Constraints table, line 100; Focus Area 6
assumption_register.verification_method: Open generated .md files in Obsidian, verify wikilinks resolve and frontmatter renders

assumption_register.assumption: AGPL-3 licensed dependencies are acceptable (contradicts own license rule)
assumption_register.mitigation: chromem-go has MIT dual-license option. coder/hnsw does NOT have a permissive alternative.
assumption_register.risk_if_false: Distribution of AGPL-3 code in a closed-source binary may require releasing all source code
assumption_register.source: Approved Dependencies table, lines 109-110 vs LICENSE RULE line 123
assumption_register.verification_method: Legal review of AGPL-3 obligations for desktop binary distribution

assumption_register.assumption: The internal/memory/**, internal/graphstore/** paths will exist for auto-activation
assumption_register.mitigation: Paths are defined by the implementation plan itself; they will exist after first implementation wave
assumption_register.risk_if_false: Auto-activation never fires; agent only reachable via explicit triggers
assumption_register.verification_method: Verify paths exist after Layer 0 implementation

commendations: Exceptionally well-researched sharp edges (25 pitfalls) with real-world references (lnd PR #6547 for bbolt mmap bug, sqlite-vec README incompatibility). This is the most thorough sharp edges file in the ecosystem.
CORRECT/WRONG example pattern used consistently across all 11 Focus Areas, matching the established go-pro and go-tui convention exactly.

Bi-temporal edge design (valid_time + transaction_time) is architecturally sophisticated — provides both 'what was true when' and 'what did the system believe when' queries, which is critical for a memory system that must handle corrections.
Approved Dependencies table with explicit license annotations is a pattern other agents should adopt. The license awareness (even with the AGPL gap) shows mature engineering judgment.

Parallelization section with Layer 0-4 dependency graph is clear, actionable, and correctly identifies all dependencies — types before interfaces, interfaces before implementations, everything before tests.
The dual connection pool pattern (readDB + writeDB) for SQLite WAL mode is the correct architecture and is documented with clear rationale.
The fsnotify sharp edges (duplicate events, no recursive watch, write feedback loop) demonstrate awareness of real-world deployment issues that would otherwise burn implementation hours.

bottlenecks: agents-index.json integration is a serial bottleneck — all downstream workflows (routing, auto-activation, team-run, /implement) depend on it
The license decision on coder/hnsw blocks the vector search architecture: must decide between cap-at-100K, find alternative, or accept AGPL before Focus Area 10 implementation
circular_risks: No circular dependencies detected. The Layer 0-4 dependency graph is acyclic.
Minor concern: vault sync (Layer 2) writes markdown that entity extraction (Layer 2) could read, but these are in separate packages with no import cycle.
critical_path: agents-index.json entry → identity injection → trigger routing → /implement workflow
License resolution (C-1) → dependency finalization → implementation start
Convention separation (M-2) → context_requirements config → team-run injection

executive_assessment.summary: The go-db-architect agent demonstrates deep domain expertise and comprehensive sharp edges coverage, but has one license contradiction (AGPL dependency violates its own GPL prohibition), is significantly implementation-heavier than ecosystem peers (882 lines vs 374-471 peer average), and cannot be deployed without an agents-index.json entry and file relocation. The core design is sound; the conditions are structural, not conceptual.

failure_mode_analysis.detection: No routing to go-db-architect despite matching triggers; go-pro handles all Go work
failure_mode_analysis.mitigation: Block deployment until agents-index.json entry is created and validated with gogent-validate

failure_mode_analysis.detection: License audit or legal review discovers AGPL obligation
failure_mode_analysis.mitigation: Resolve C-1 before any implementation begins. Add go-licenses check to CI pipeline.
failure_mode_analysis.scenario: coder/hnsw AGPL-3 dependency introduced and discovered post-release

failure_mode_analysis.detection: Agent produces lower quality output due to context pressure; truncation warnings
failure_mode_analysis.mitigation: Factor into agent identity + db-conventions.md (M-2). Monitor token counts after deployment.
failure_mode_analysis.scenario: Agent identity too large for team-run context window

failure_mode_analysis.detection: go-pro produces generic Go code instead of domain-specific memory system code
failure_mode_analysis.mitigation: Configure auto_activate paths in agents-index.json. Path-based activation takes priority over trigger matching.
failure_mode_analysis.scenario: Trigger collision: 'implement the memory system' routes to go-pro instead of go-db-architect

handoff_notes: Beethoven should focus on: (1) The convention separation question — Einstein's theoretical analysis may have a different perspective on whether 882 lines is architecturally appropriate vs. the practical split into agent+convention. (2) The AGPL license issue — is there a theoretical framework for when AGPL is acceptable in desktop-distributed binaries? (3) Whether 25 sharp edges represents thoroughness or over-specification — compare with the ecosystem norm of 5-8. (4) The tension between 'implementation cookbook' and 'agent identity' — is there a clean theoretical boundary, or is the go-db-architect's approach actually superior for highly domain-specific agents where the domain knowledge IS the value?

issue_register.description: The Approved Dependencies table lists `github.com/coder/hnsw` as AGPL-3. The agent's own LICENSE RULE states: 'Never introduce GPL/SSPL dependencies. Never embed Neo4j (GPLv3).' AGPL-3 is in the GPL family (Affero General Public License) and is MORE restrictive than GPL-3, requiring source disclosure for network-accessible services. This is a direct internal contradiction.
issue_register.evidence: Line 119: `github.com/coder/hnsw | ANN vector index (>100K) | AGPL-3`. Line 123: `LICENSE RULE: Never introduce GPL/SSPL dependencies.` chromem-go is listed as 'AGPL-3 / MIT' (dual-licensed, MIT option available), but coder/hnsw is AGPL-3 only.
issue_register.impact: If an implementer follows the approved dependencies table literally, they would introduce an AGPL-3 dependency into a single-binary desktop distribution, potentially requiring the entire binary to be open-sourced under AGPL-3 terms. For a product targeting non-technical users, this is a distribution poison pill.
issue_register.recommendation: Three options: (1) Remove coder/hnsw from approved dependencies entirely and cap vector search at chromem-go's 100K limit. (2) Find an MIT/BSD-licensed HNSW alternative (e.g., a custom ~200-line implementation using gonum). (3) Explicitly carve out an AGPL exception in the license rule with a documented rationale. Option 1 is simplest if >100K vectors is not a near-term requirement.
issue_register.title: AGPL-3 dependency contradicts agent's own license rule

issue_register.description: The go-db-architect agent has no entry in agents-index.json. Without this entry, the agent cannot be: (1) routed to by the Intent Gate, (2) auto-activated by path patterns, (3) spawned by orchestrators or impl-manager, (4) used in the /implement workflow via gogent-plan-impl, (5) have its identity injected by gogent-validate or team-run. The agent effectively does not exist in the GOgent-Fortress system despite having a complete .md definition.
issue_register.evidence: Searched agents-index.json (1548 lines) — no 'go-db-architect' entry exists. The impl-manager's can_spawn list includes go-pro, go-cli, go-tui, go-api, go-concurrent but NOT go-db-architect.
issue_register.impact: Agent cannot participate in any workflow. All 20 triggers are dead. Auto-activation paths (internal/memory/**, internal/graphstore/**) are not registered. The entire agent definition is inert.
issue_register.recommendation: Create agents-index.json entry following the go-pro pattern. Required fields: id, parallelization_template (D), name, model (sonnet), thinking (true), thinking_budget (14000), tier (2), category (language), path, triggers, tools, cli_flags, auto_activate, conventions_required, sharp_edges_count (25), description, context_requirements. Also add 'go-db-architect' to impl-manager.can_spawn and orchestrator.can_spawn lists.
issue_register.title: No agents-index.json entry — agent is undiscoverable by routing system

issue_register.description: At 882 lines, go-db-architect is 2.4x larger than go-pro (374), 1.9x larger than go-tui (471), and 2.3x larger than go-cli (389). The excess comes from ~400 lines of complete Go function implementations and a 90-line SQL schema. Peer agents provide patterns and rules (CORRECT/WRONG examples); go-db-architect provides copy-pasteable implementations including full function bodies for decayScore(), compositeScore(), reciprocalRankFusion(), LoadSubgraph(), RunMigrations(), WriteEntityFile(), etc.
issue_register.evidence: Focus Areas 1-11 each contain 30-60 lines of Go code with complete function signatures, bodies, and SQL queries. Compare go-pro Focus Area 2 (Error Handling): 15 lines showing patterns. go-db-architect Focus Area 7 (Consolidation): 35 lines of complete algorithm implementation.
issue_register.impact: Token inflation: agent identity injection adds ~18K tokens to context (per memory note on identity injection fix). With ~6K of that being full implementations the agent may never use, this is wasted context budget. Additionally, copy-paste implementations may not adapt well to the actual codebase structure that emerges during implementation.
issue_register.recommendation: Factor into two files: (1) go-db-architect.md (~400 lines): Agent identity, role description, system constraints, approved dependencies, rules per focus area (CORRECT/WRONG patterns, not full implementations), testing strategy, output requirements, parallelization. (2) db-conventions.md (~400 lines): Full SQL schema, complete function implementations, algorithm details, template strings. Wire db-conventions.md via context_requirements.conventions.base in agents-index.json. This matches the go-tui pattern: agent identity in go-tui.md, framework patterns in go-bubbletea.md convention.
issue_register.title: Agent is implementation-cookbook, not agent-identity — 2.4x peer size

issue_register.description: The agent definition and sharp edges live in tickets/obsidian-cli-knowledge-graph/ which is a ticket working directory. Deployed agents live in ~/.claude/agents/{agent-id}/. The identity injection system (pkg/routing/identity_loader.go) reads from ~/.claude/agents/{id}/{id}.md. Team-run's prepareSpawn also resolves agent identity from the agents/ directory.
issue_register.evidence: Current paths: tickets/obsidian-cli-knowledge-graph/go-db-architect.md and tickets/obsidian-cli-knowledge-graph/go-db-architect-sharp-edges.yaml. Expected paths: ~/.claude/agents/go-db-architect/go-db-architect.md and ~/.claude/agents/go-db-architect/sharp-edges.yaml.
issue_register.impact: Even with an agents-index.json entry, the identity injection system won't find the .md file. The agent will run without its identity (the exact bug fixed by the identity injection fix in memory). Sharp edges won't be loaded by gogent-validate's auto_inject mechanism.
issue_register.recommendation: Move files to correct locations: mkdir -p ~/.claude/agents/go-db-architect/ && mv tickets/obsidian-cli-knowledge-graph/go-db-architect.md ~/.claude/agents/go-db-architect/go-db-architect.md && mv tickets/obsidian-cli-knowledge-graph/go-db-architect-sharp-edges.yaml ~/.claude/agents/go-db-architect/sharp-edges.yaml
issue_register.title: File location is wrong — tickets/ instead of agents/

issue_register.description: Several domain-specific acronyms are used without definition: FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler), STM (Short-Term Memory), LTM (Long-Term Memory), RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion), BFS (Breadth-First Search), CTE (Common Table Expression), FTS5 (Full-Text Search version 5), WAL (Write-Ahead Logging). While some are common (BFS, WAL), others are domain-specific (FSRS, RRF) and would confuse a contractor unfamiliar with spaced repetition or information retrieval.
issue_register.evidence: Line 535: 'FSRS power-law' — no expansion. Line 564: 'STM → LTM promotion' — no expansion. Line 336: 'Reciprocal Rank Fusion (k=60)' — defined here but 'RRF' used elsewhere without reference.
issue_register.impact: Contractor confusion; potential misimplementation of decay scoring if FSRS formula origin is not understood.
issue_register.recommendation: Add a glossary section or expand each acronym on first use. FSRS in particular should include a citation or link to the paper.
issue_register.title: Domain acronyms undefined on first use

issue_register.description: The frontmatter defines 5 thinking budget variants: budget (14000), budget_schema_design (18000), budget_query_optimization (18000), budget_debug (18000), budget_migration (16000). The agents-index.json schema only supports a single thinking_budget field (see go-pro: 10000, go-tui: 12000). The extra granularity is compile-time metadata only — runtime has no mechanism to select different budgets per task type.
issue_register.evidence: go-pro frontmatter: budget (10000), budget_refactor (14000), budget_debug (18000) — only 'budget' maps to agents-index.json thinking_budget. go-db-architect adds budget_schema_design and budget_query_optimization which have no precedent.
issue_register.impact: Low — the extra fields are ignored at runtime. But they set false expectations about per-task budget adjustment.
issue_register.recommendation: Keep the three standard fields (budget, budget_refactor, budget_debug) for consistency. The schema_design and query_optimization cases can map to budget_debug (18000) which is the 'deep thinking' tier. Alternatively, add a comment noting these are authoring hints, not runtime configuration.
issue_register.title: Thinking budget granularity won't map to agents-index.json

issue_register.description: go-db-architect has 20 triggers, some overlapping with existing agents. 'memory system' and 'memory retrieval' could match memory-archivist's 'save memory' context. 'embedding' is generic enough to match go-api or python-pro contexts. The trigger resolution priority rules in CLAUDE.md (file-type auto-activation > language-qualified > specific > generic) should resolve most cases via path-based auto-activation, but only if auto_activate paths are registered in agents-index.json.
issue_register.evidence: go-db-architect triggers: 'memory system', 'memory retrieval', 'embedding', 'consolidation'. memory-archivist triggers: 'save memory', 'capture learnings'. go-pro triggers: 'implement', 'refactor', 'optimize'. Without agents-index.json auto_activate registration, path-based disambiguation is inoperable.
issue_register.impact: Incorrect routing: 'implement the memory system' could route to go-pro instead of go-db-architect. Mitigated by auto_activate path patterns once agents-index.json is configured.
issue_register.recommendation: Ensure agents-index.json auto_activate paths are configured before deploying triggers. Consider prefixing ambiguous triggers: 'graph schema' is unambiguous; 'memory system' could be qualified as 'memory graph system' or 'memory subsystem schema'.
issue_register.title: Trigger overlap risk with go-pro and memory-archivist

issue_register.description: The Testing Strategy section shows patterns for SQLite (in-memory DB, benchmarks, bi-temporal queries) but omits test patterns for: (1) bbolt cache layer — how to test cache hit/miss, invalidation, compaction. (2) Vault sync — how to test fsnotify event handling, debouncing, feedback loop prevention. (3) Entity extraction — how to mock LLM output for deterministic testing.
issue_register.evidence: Lines 773-807: Only SQLite testing patterns shown. No mention of bbolt test setup, vault test directory patterns, or LLM output fixture approach.
issue_register.impact: Contractor may skip testing for 3 of the 11 focus areas. bbolt byte-lifetime bugs (sharp edge bbolt-byte-slice-lifetime) are exactly the kind of issue caught by tests, not code review.
issue_register.recommendation: Add 3 test pattern examples: (1) bbolt: `bbolt.Open(':memory:', ...)` or temp file with t.Cleanup; test cache invalidation with generation counters. (2) vault: t.TempDir() for vault root, manual fsnotify event injection, content-hash comparison for feedback loop prevention. (3) LLM extraction: JSON fixture files with known-good and malformed outputs; test defensive parser against both.
issue_register.title: Testing section missing patterns for bbolt, vault sync, and LLM extraction

issue_register.description: The Parallelization section (lines 826-869) prescribes 'Types and interfaces before implementations' with Layer 1 defining GraphStore interface, CacheLayer interface, VectorIndex interface, VaultSync interface. But the Focus Areas (lines 128-762) provide concrete struct implementations (GraphStore struct, CacheLayer struct) without first showing the interfaces they should implement. The agent doesn't model the pattern it advocates.
issue_register.evidence: Line 231: 'type GraphStore struct { readDB *sql.DB; writeDB *sql.DB }' — concrete struct, no interface shown. Line 383: 'type CacheLayer struct { db *bbolt.DB; generation uint64 }' — concrete struct. Lines 836-837: 'internal/graphstore/store.go — GraphStore interface' and 'internal/graphstore/cache.go — CacheLayer interface' — interfaces prescribed but never defined.
issue_register.impact: Low — a competent Go developer will extract interfaces naturally. But for contractor readiness, showing the interface definitions would prevent ambiguity about the contract boundary.
issue_register.recommendation: Add a brief Interface Contracts section between System Constraints and Focus Areas, showing the key interfaces (GraphStore, CacheLayer, VectorIndex, VaultSync) with their method signatures. This becomes Layer 1 in the parallelization model and guides the contractor before they see implementations.
issue_register.title: Agent definition contains concrete schema but preaches interface-first

high_priority.action: Resolve AGPL-3 license contradiction (C-1): Remove coder/hnsw from approved dependencies OR find MIT/BSD alternative OR document explicit AGPL carve-out
high_priority.effort: 30 minutes for decision, 0-2 hours for implementation depending on choice
high_priority.rationale: Blocks all implementation. Cannot proceed with ambiguous licensing on a desktop-distributed binary.

high_priority.action: Create agents-index.json entry (M-1): Add complete go-db-architect entry following go-pro template, add to impl-manager.can_spawn and orchestrator.can_spawn
high_priority.rationale: Agent is inert without this. All workflows (routing, auto-activation, team-run, /implement) depend on it.

high_priority.action: Move files to correct location (M-3): ~/.claude/agents/go-db-architect/{go-db-architect.md, sharp-edges.yaml}
high_priority.rationale: Identity injection system reads from agents/ directory. Current location in tickets/ is invisible to runtime.

low_priority.action: Define domain acronyms (m-1): Expand FSRS, STM, LTM, RRF on first use
low_priority.rationale: Contractor readiness; prevents misunderstanding of decay scoring algorithm origins

low_priority.action: Normalize thinking budget fields (m-2): Use standard budget/budget_refactor/budget_debug triple
low_priority.rationale: Consistency with peer agents; extra fields are runtime-inert

low_priority.action: Add Interface Contracts section (m-5): Define GraphStore, CacheLayer, VectorIndex, VaultSync interfaces
low_priority.rationale: Models the interface-first pattern the agent itself advocates; clarifies contracts for contractor

medium_priority.action: Factor into agent identity + db-conventions.md (M-2): Move SQL schemas, complete function implementations, and algorithm details to ~/.claude/conventions/db-conventions.md. Keep patterns, rules, and CORRECT/WRONG examples in agent .md.
medium_priority.effort: 2-3 hours for careful separation and testing
medium_priority.rationale: Reduces agent identity from ~882 to ~400 lines, matching peer agents. Moves implementation details to convention layer where they can be conditionally loaded.

medium_priority.action: Add test patterns for bbolt, vault sync, and LLM extraction (m-4)
medium_priority.rationale: 3 of 11 focus areas lack testing guidance. bbolt byte-lifetime bugs are exactly the class of issue caught by tests.

conditions: C-1 resolved: AGPL-3 license contradiction eliminated (coder/hnsw removed, replaced, or explicitly excepted)
M-1 addressed: agents-index.json entry created with all required fields
post_approval_monitoring: Monitor token consumption after deployment — if agent identity + conventions exceeds 20K tokens, the convention separation (M-2) becomes urgent
Watch for trigger collisions with go-pro on 'memory' and 'embedding' keywords — check routing logs after first 10 invocations
Verify chromem-go performance claim (A-3) with benchmark at 100K vectors before committing to it as the sole <100K solution
After first implementation wave, verify auto_activate paths (internal/memory/**, internal/graphstore/**) correctly trigger go-db-architect over go-pro
